title: Inside Google Antigravity 2.0: The complete developer guide | The Agent Factory
summary: Google Antigravity 2.0 is an agentic platform that orchestrates specialized sub-agents for software development tasks. It unbundled from a simple IDE into a full ecosystem with a desktop agent manager, CLI, advanced SDK, and custom IDE. The platform enables developers to use voice prompts and skills to coordinate parallel agents for building, localizing, and deploying applications.
writeup: An interview with Roddy Davis, a lead engineer on Google Antigravity (formerly Firebase Studio), on how expert engineers integrate agentic tools into daily practice without surrendering judgment. Davis covers his workflow across the Antigravity IDE, CLI, SDK, and agent manager, with particular depth on the role of skills, architecture discipline, and selective code review. The through-line is a concrete, practitioner-level argument that flat architectures and well-scoped context make agents faster to steer and easier to audit.
key_points: Skills in Antigravity compress reusable context into triggerable workflows: Davis maintains global skills (Obsidian, Flutter, Firebase) and per-project skills (design systems such as Shadcn) so the model inherits the right type vocabulary without lengthy prompts on every run.

Davis favors flat architectures where state, UI, and data layers are strictly separated, not because it is easier, but because deviations are immediately visible, letting the engineer steer the agent back on track before drift compounds.

Code review effort scales with stakes: HTML/CSS for a marketing site gets a visual pass only, while Go backend API contracts and ABI interfaces receive deep scrutiny because a schema change cascades to every downstream consumer.

A high-leverage review pattern is writing the first one or two implementations by hand, then letting the agent extend the pattern; this eliminates guessing about what the agent will produce because it is copying an existing example.

MCP servers provide tool-level context that changes output quality: Davis keeps a Flutter/Dart MCP server always enabled in Antigravity so the agent can trigger hot reloads and DevTools rather than reasoning about them abstractly.

Davis used Antigravity to build a macOS Finder extension in Swift and a Chrome extension, both from a single prompt, illustrating that agents can now target platform-native targets a typical engineer would avoid due to unfamiliarity with the language.
